Small Actions,
Big Impact

Civics & Community Engagement — A Real Story

University of Chakwal  ·  2025
Civic Responsibility

It starts with noticing.

Civic responsibility isn't always grand campaigns or large organisations. It begins with paying attention — to the people around you, the spaces you inhabit, and the quiet needs that go unspoken. Two moments of noticing led to two initiatives that changed something small but real.

🌳

Environment

Greening shared spaces for the community

🍱

Welfare

Supporting dignified individuals in quiet need

🤝

Consistency

Small actions repeated make lasting change

Story One · Plantation Drive

Roots in the Neighbourhood

The area around our local mosque sat bare and sun-baked. No shade, no greenery — just open ground people crossed quickly. Someone said, half-jokingly: "We should plant a tree." The joke became a plan.

We planted 7–8 trees including fast-growing shade varieties, added flower beds bordered with white stone, and arranged potted roses and marigolds along the walkway.

This was not an individual effort. All five group members contributed together — from arranging plants and resources to physically planting and maintaining them. Each member played an active role in turning a simple idea into a shared community initiative.

"A planted tree doesn't give shade immediately. But it's a promise — that someone thought about tomorrow."
Story One · In Pictures

The Plantation Drive

From bare ground to green shade — a neighbourhood transformed.

Young saplings marked with stakes
Shade trees taking root in the plot
Potted roses and marigolds at the entrance
Story Two · Meal Initiative

Not Charity —
Shared Humanity

When fuel prices rose sharply, daily life became harder for minimum-wage workers — those who maintain dignity and don't ask for help. We decided to respond quietly.

22 meal boxes every 14 days, home-cooked, packed fresh, distributed with care and without fanfare.

This initiative is a collective effort of all five group members. We contribute financially and in resources, but more importantly, we share responsibility. The meals are prepared in a rotational system — each time, a different member's home takes the responsibility of cooking. Every 14 days, the cycle continues, ensuring consistency, fairness, and shared participation.

"Not a photo op. Just two people acknowledging each other's humanity."
Who received the meals
🛒

Street Vendors

Working roadside markets all day

📱

SIM Card Sellers

Long shifts at roadside counters

🔒

Security Guards

Overnight shifts, away from family

🏠

Workers Far from Home

Eating alone, quietly getting by

The People Behind the Work

Department of Civics & Community Engagement

All five members actively contributed—from funding and planning to execution. Both plantation and meal distribution were carried out as shared responsibilities, with a rotating system for meal preparation.

University of Chakwal  ·  Est. 2020

Civic life begins with
small, honest acts.

We didn't set out to change the world. We planted a few trees and cooked a few meals. But we learned that consistency, care, and showing up for your community — even quietly — is what citizenship actually looks like.
